items.correct: A verb in the subjunctive mood.
items.why: Students use infinitive after impersonal expressions. 'Il faut que tu PARTES' (subjunctive), not 'il faut que tu partir'. The que triggers subjunctive in the following clause.

items.q: Which mood follows 'je souhaite que' (I wish that)?
items.why: Students use indicative after expressions of wish. SOUHAITER QUE, VOULOIR QUE, AIMER QUE all trigger subjunctive when there's a different subject. Note: espérer que typically takes indicative - a common irregularity.
Sensible is a faux ami. French 'sensible' = sensitive/easily affected. English 'sensible' = raisonnable in French. Confusion leads to wrong impressions of someone's character.
The answer examiners want is: Simile. “Metaphor” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.correct: The opposite of what is meant
items.why: Correct answer: The opposite of what is meant. “Exactly what is meant” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: How many key figures of speech are introduced in this stamp?
items.why: Exact answer: 6. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
